task: I’m reviewing a supplier lot that has a few borderline parts mixed in, and I need a simple way to decide what gets accepted, what gets set aside, and what needs a formal write-up. Can you help me sketch a practical checklist for handling it without letting a bad batch slip through?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a simple, practical checklist you can use to sort a mixed supplier lot without letting a bad batch slip through.

## Quick triage checklist

### 1) Identify the lot
- Supplier name
- Lot / batch number
- Part number / revision
- Quantity received
- Date received
- Inspector name

### 2) Check the basic requirements
Compare the lot against:
- Drawing / spec
- Purchase order
- Approved sample / golden part
- Any special customer or regulatory requirements

If any of these are missing or unclear, **pause and escalate**.

### 3) Sort parts into 3 buckets

#### Accept
Put in this bucket if the part:
- Meets all critical and major requirements
- Falls within tolerance
- Has no visible damage, contamination, or mix-up risk
- Matches the correct revision / marking / packaging
- Has no unresolved documentation issues

#### Set aside / hold
Use this for parts that are:
- Borderline but not clearly nonconforming
- Missing minor paperwork or traceability details
- Suspect due to packaging damage or handling concerns
- Needing a second inspection or measurement
- Potentially acceptable only if engineering/quality confirms

Keep these physically separated and clearly labeled:
- “HOLD”
- Do not use
- Do not ship

#### Formal write-up required
Create a nonconformance / deviation report if the part:
- Misses a critical dimension or functional requirement
- Shows repeated borderline results across multiple parts
- Suggests a pattern or lot-level issue
- Could affect safety, fit, function, compliance, or customer acceptance
- Is outside any allowed tolerance or approved deviation

### 4) Decide if it’s a part issue or a lot issue
Ask:
- Is this one isolated part?
- Are several parts showing the same problem?
- Is the issue random or systematic?
- Could the whole lot be suspect?

If the same defect appears more than once, treat it as **lot risk**, not just a single-part problem.

### 5) Verify containment
Before releasing anything:
- Separate accepted parts from held parts
- Tag questionable parts immediately
- Record counts for each category
- Prevent commingling with good inventory
- Notify receiving, quality, and production if needed

### 6) Document the decision
For each category, record:
- What was checked
- What was found
- Why it was accepted, held, or rejected
- Who made the decision
- Any follow-up action needed

### 7) Escalation triggers
Stop and escalate if:
- The defect is safety-related
- The same issue repeats
- There’s uncertainty about the spec
- The lot may be mixed with another revision or supplier source
- The disposition could affect customer delivery

## Simple rule of thumb
- **Accept** = clearly meets requirements
- **Set aside** = uncertain, needs review, but not proven bad
- **Formal write-up** = nonconforming, repeated, or lot-impacting issue

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page inspection form or a decision tree you can use at receiving.

user_simulator.text:
Close, but I’d want this tightened up a bit. Right now it’s still a little broad for a mixed lot.

What I need is a cleaner decision rule, something like:
- **accept** only when it passes spec and traceability
- **hold** when it’s borderline or needs a second check
- **write-up** when it’s out of spec, repeated, or the lot is suspect

A few things to add:
1. Define what counts as **borderline** versus just bad.
2. Say who gets the final call on held parts.
3. Add a hard stop for **mixed revision / mixed lot / missing traceability**.
4. Include a note on **sample size** if only part of the lot is checked.

If you can turn it into a one-page receiving checklist with check boxes, that’d be better. Keep it practical — no theory.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, practical **one-page receiving checklist** you can use for a mixed supplier lot.

---

# Receiving Checklist: Mixed Lot Triage

**Lot / PO:** ____________  **Supplier:** ____________  **Part No.:** ____________  **Rev.:** ____________  
**Qty Received:** ____________  **Date:** ____________  **Inspector:** ____________

## 1) Hard stop checks
If **any** of these are true, **stop and do not accept the lot**:

- [ ] **Mixed revision** found
- [ ] **Mixed lot / mixed source** found
- [ ] **Missing traceability** (lot number, cert, traveler, label, or required ID)
- [ ] **Wrong part number / revision**
- [ ] **Packaging or labeling does not match paperwork**
- [ ] **No clear way to link parts to the received lot**

**Action:**  
- [ ] Hold all affected parts  
- [ ] Notify Quality / Receiving Lead  
- [ ] Open formal write-up if needed

---

## 2) Define the part status

### Accept
Check **Accept** only if the part:
- [ ] Meets spec / drawing / PO requirements
- [ ] Meets traceability requirements
- [ ] Matches correct part number and revision
- [ ] Has no visible damage or mix-up risk
- [ ] Has no unresolved paperwork issue

### Hold
Check **Hold** if the part is:
- [ ] Borderline on a measurement or visual feature
- [ ] Not clearly bad, but not clearly good
- [ ] Needs a second measurement or second inspector
- [ ] Missing minor supporting info, but traceability is still intact
- [ ] Suspect due to packaging, handling, or possible mix-up

**Borderline =** close to the limit, unclear by one inspection, or needing confirmation.  
**Bad =** clearly out of spec, clearly wrong, or traceability is broken.

### Write-up required
Check **Write-up required** if the part or lot is:
- [ ] Out of spec
- [ ] Repeatedly borderline across multiple parts
- [ ] Showing the same issue more than once
- [ ] Suspect as a lot, not just one part
- [ ] Affecting fit, function, safety, or customer requirement

---

## 3) Sample size / inspection coverage
If only part of the lot is checked:

- [ ] Sample size recorded: ____________
- [ ] Sampling method recorded
- [ ] Sample is representative of the lot
- [ ] If one failure is found, expand inspection
- [ ] If multiple borderline parts appear, treat lot as suspect

**Rule:**  
If the sample shows a pattern, do **not** assume the rest is good.

---

## 4) Final disposition

### Accept
- [ ] All checked parts pass
- [ ] Traceability is complete
- [ ] No mix-up risk

### Hold
- [ ] Borderline parts separated
- [ ] Tagged “HOLD – DO NOT USE”
- [ ] Sent to final decision owner

### Write-up
- [ ] Nonconformance / deviation opened
- [ ] Affected quantity estimated
- [ ] Lot containment started

---

## 5) Final call on held parts
**Held parts are not released by the receiving inspector alone.**  
Final disposition goes to:

- [ ] Quality
- [ ] Engineering
- [ ] Supplier Quality
- [ ] Designated MRB / review authority

**Final decision made by:** ____________________  **Date:** ____________

---

## 6) Containment actions
- [ ] Accepted parts segregated from held parts
- [ ] Held parts clearly labeled
- [ ] No commingling with good stock
- [ ] Production notified if needed
- [ ] Supplier notified if lot issue is suspected

---

## Quick decision rule
- **Accept** = passes spec + traceability
- **Hold** = borderline or needs second check
- **Write-up** = out of spec, repeated, or lot suspect
- **Hard stop** = mixed revision, mixed lot, or missing traceability

If you want, I can turn this into a cleaner **checkbox form layout** you can paste directly into Word or Excel.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. Clean enough to use.

The hard stop on mixed revision / mixed lot / missing traceability is the part I wanted. Borderline vs bad is clear, and the sample-size note is decent too.

Only thing I’d keep in mind on the floor: if the sample turns up one real miss, I’d expand the check before anybody gets comfortable with the rest of the lot. But as a one-page receiving checklist, this works.
